```
/**
 * Export script — renders slides to PNG using Playwright.
 *
 * Usage:
 *   npm run export                    — export with current config
 *   npm run export -- --out=./output  — custom output directory
 *   npm run export -- --width=1920    — custom width (height = width / (16/9))
 */

import { chromium } from 'playwright';
import { mkdir } from 'fs/promises';
import { resolve, join } from 'path';
import { fileURLToPath } from 'url';

const __dirname = fileURLToPath(new URL('.', import.meta.url));
const PROJECT_ROOT = resolve(__dirname, '..');

// Parse CLI args
const args = Object.fromEntries(
  process.argv.slice(2)
    .filter(a => a.startsWith('--'))
    .map(a => {
      const [k, v] = a.slice(2).split('=');
      return [k, v || 'true'];
    })
);

const OUTPUT_DIR = resolve(PROJECT_ROOT, args.out || 'output');
const WIDTH = parseInt(args.width || '1920', 10);
const HEIGHT = Math.round(WIDTH / (16 / 9));
const DEV_SERVER = args.url || 'http://localhost:5173';

async function main() {
  await mkdir(OUTPUT_DIR, { recursive: true });

  console.log(`\nExporting slides at ${WIDTH}x${HEIGHT}`);
  console.log(`Output: ${OUTPUT_DIR}\n`);

  const browser = await chromium.launch();
  const page = await browser.newPage({
    viewport: { width: WIDTH, height: HEIGHT },
    deviceScaleFactor: 2,  // 2x for crisp output
  });

  await page.goto(DEV_SERVER, { waitUntil: 'networkidle' });

  // Wait for fonts to load
  await page.evaluate(() => document.fonts.ready);
  // Extra settle time for font rendering
  await page.waitForTimeout(500);

  // Find all slides
  const slideIds = await page.$$eval('.slide', els => els.map(el => el.id));
  console.log(`Found ${slideIds.length} slides\n`);

  for (const id of slideIds) {
    const slideEl = page.locator(`#${id}`);
    const filename = `${id}.png`;
    const filepath = join(OUTPUT_DIR, filename);

    await slideEl.screenshot({ path: filepath });
    console.log(`  Exported: ${filename}`);
  }

  await browser.close();
  console.log(`\nDone. ${slideIds.length} slides exported to ${OUTPUT_DIR}\n`);
}

main().catch(err => {
  console.error('Export failed:', err);
  process.exit(1);
});
```
</details></tool-use>

```
import { defineConfig } from 'vite';
import fs from 'fs';
import path from 'path';
import sharp from 'sharp';

/** Helper: backup a file with a timestamp, then write new data */
function backupAndWrite(filePath, data, ts) {
  const ext = path.extname(filePath);
  const base = path.basename(filePath, ext);
  const dir = path.dirname(filePath);
  const backupPath = path.join(dir, `${base}_${ts}${ext}`);
  if (fs.existsSync(filePath)) {
    fs.copyFileSync(filePath, backupPath);
  }
  fs.writeFileSync(filePath, JSON.stringify(data, null, 2) + '\n');
  return path.basename(backupPath);
}

/** Vite plugin: save content.json + config.json via POST /api/save */
function savePlugin() {
  return {
    name: 'deck-save',
    configureServer(server) {
      // Keep the old endpoint working
      server.middlewares.use('/api/save-content', async (req, res) => {
        if (req.method !== 'POST') { res.statusCode = 405; res.end('Method not allowed'); return; }
        let body = ''; for await (const chunk of req) body += chunk;
        try {
          const json = JSON.parse(body);
          const ts = new Date().toISOString().replace(/[:.]/g, '-').slice(0, 19);
          const backup = backupAndWrite(path.resolve('content.json'), json, ts);
          res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json');
          res.end(JSON.stringify({ ok: true, backup }));
        } catch (e) { res.statusCode = 400; res.end(JSON.stringify({ error: e.message })); }
      });

      // Save a PNG slide image to disk
      server.middlewares.use('/api/save-slide', async (req, res) => {
        if (req.method !== 'POST') { res.statusCode = 405; res.end('Method not allowed'); return; }
        const chunks = [];
        for await (const chunk of req) chunks.push(chunk);
        const body = Buffer.concat(chunks);
        try {
          // Expect JSON with { filename, dataUrl }
          const { filename, dataUrl, subdir } = JSON.parse(body.toString());
          const exportDir = subdir
            ? path.resolve('export', subdir)
            : path.resolve('export');
          if (!fs.existsSync(exportDir)) fs.mkdirSync(exportDir, { recursive: true });
          const base64 = dataUrl.replace(/^data:image\/png;base64,/, '');

          // Lossless PNG optimization via sharp — better compression, same pixels

            .png({ compressionLevel: 9, adaptiveFiltering: true })

          fs.writeFileSync(filePath, optimized);

          res.end(JSON.stringify({ ok: true, path: `export/${filename}`, saved: `${saved}%` }));

                x: 0, y: 0, w: '100%', h: '100%',
              });
            }

              const stream = fs.createWriteStream(path.join(dir, 'presentation.pdf'));
              doc.pipe(stream);
              for (const png of pngs) {

                doc.image(path.join(dir, png), 0, 0, { width: pageW, height: pageH });
```

The timestamped `content_2026-04-03T09-25-11.json` is not the live deck. The save plugin copies the old file into a backup and then writes the new state into `content.json`, so `content.json` and `config.json` are the current source of truth the app renders. I’m pulling out the deck structure and the current visual config so we have a working mental model of the active presentation, not just the backups.

_**Agent (gpt-5.4 2026-04-03 09:30:23Z)**_

**Source Of Truth**

The app’s live source is `[content.json](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/content.json)` plus `[config.json](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/config.json)`. `src/main.js` fetches those directly at [src/main.js](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/src/main.js#L4), and Vite saves by backing up the old file first, then writing the new live file at [vite.config.js](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/vite.config.js#L7) and [vite.config.js](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/vite.config.js#L139). That means `content_2026-04-03T09-25-11.json` is a pre-save backup, not the deck currently used by the app.

I verified the live preview in the browser. The current app renders 65 slides from the April 3, 2026 live JSON. The exported decks in [export/16x9](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/export/16x9) and [export/4x3](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/export/4x3) are older March 30 outputs and still reflect the previous 64-slide deck.

**Content Map**

The deck’s thesis is coherent and strong: AI in higher education is no longer mainly a tool question; it is becoming an infrastructure-control question. The active cover and closing question in [content.json](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/content.json) frame that clearly.

The current story arc is:

1. Opening: cover, then three intro cards (`Dr Stuart Grey`, `Distributed Agents for Autonomous Spacecraft`, `Free Text Analysis with Student Voice AI`), then `Transparency`, `Distortion`, `Extraction`, then the main thesis statement.
2. `From tool to substrate`: AI shifts from visible tool to invisible system; governance lags; dependence becomes visible through failure.
3. `The build-out`: the boom creates real capacity, but concentrated ownership and lock-in risks.
4. `Who sets the defaults?`: concentrated firms shape the stack; procurement becomes mission drift.
5. `What dependence does`: the augmentation/substitution divide, pedagogy bending to platforms, data extraction, inequality.
6. `The lifecycle of capture`: generosity, then squeeze, then expensive exit.
7. `A different strategy`: the constructive turn; stewardship, governance, procurement, evaluation, modularity, collaboration.
8. `What the next decade looks like`: two futures, three decisions, Monday-morning agenda, closing question.

Structurally, the deck is heavy on argument slides: 37 `content`, 12 `statement`, 5 `data`, 10 `section-divider`, 1 `cover`. The longest and most actionable section is `A different strategy`; that is where the deck stops diagnosing and starts telling leaders what to do.

**Editorial Read**

The deck is not anti-AI. Its stance is: use the moment, but do not surrender control. The strongest repeated framing is `access vs control`, `augmentation vs substitution`, and `infrastructure before debate`. That gives the deck a clear ideological center.

The main content risks I see are:

- The opening is long before the first evidence slide. There are 8 slides before `From tool to substrate` starts, and 6 of them are pure framing.
- The deck is rhetorically strong but dense. Many sections use the same rhythm: thesis slide, supporting slides, takeaway. That helps coherence but can feel repetitive across 65 slides.
- The `data` slides carry major claims, but the JSON has no source or notes fields. If we tighten this deck for delivery, citations or speaker-note sourcing should be added somewhere.
- The intro credential cards use `section-divider` slides with empty subtitles, so they read more like standalone title cards than integrated narrative slides.
- There is some small content hygiene to fix later, like inconsistent `Takeaway` casing inside the statement slide metadata.

I’m now oriented enough to work directly on improvements in the live deck. The highest-leverage next moves would be tightening the opening, reducing repetition in the middle sections, or sharpening the final operational agenda depending on what you want the talk to do in the room.

---

```
name: "doc"
description: "Use when the task involves reading, creating, or editing `.docx` documents, especially when formatting or layout fidelity matters; prefer `python-docx` plus the bundled `scripts/render_docx.py` for visual checks."
---


# DOCX Skill

## When to use
- Read or review DOCX content where layout matters (tables, diagrams, pagination).
- Create or edit DOCX files with professional formatting.
- Validate visual layout before delivery.

## Workflow
1. Prefer visual review (layout, tables, diagrams).
   - If `soffice` and `pdftoppm` are available, convert DOCX -> PDF -> PNGs.
   - Or use `scripts/render_docx.py` (requires `pdf2image` and Poppler).
   - If these tools are missing, install them or ask the user to review rendered pages locally.
2. Use `python-docx` for edits and structured creation (headings, styles, tables, lists).
3. After each meaningful change, re-render and inspect the pages.
4. If visual review is not possible, extract text with `python-docx` as a fallback and call out layout risk.
5. Keep intermediate outputs organized and clean up after final approval.

## Temp and output conventions
- Use `tmp/docs/` for intermediate files; delete when done.
- Write final artifacts under `output/doc/` when working in this repo.
- Keep filenames stable and descriptive.

## Dependencies (install if missing)
Prefer `uv` for dependency management.

Python packages:
```
uv pip install python-docx pdf2image
```
If `uv` is unavailable:
```
python3 -m pip install python-docx pdf2image
```
System tools (for rendering):
```
# macOS (Homebrew)
brew install libreoffice poppler

# Ubuntu/Debian
sudo apt-get install -y libreoffice poppler-utils
```

If installation isn't possible in this environment, tell the user which dependency is missing and how to install it locally.

## Environment
No required environment variables.

## Rendering commands
DOCX -> PDF:
```
soffice -env:UserInstallation=file:///tmp/lo_profile_$$ --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir $OUTDIR $INPUT_DOCX
```

PDF -> PNGs:
```
pdftoppm -png $OUTDIR/$BASENAME.pdf $OUTDIR/$BASENAME
```

Bundled helper:
```
python3 scripts/render_docx.py /path/to/file.docx --output_dir /tmp/docx_pages
```

## Quality expectations
- Deliver a client-ready document: consistent typography, spacing, margins, and clear hierarchy.
- Avoid formatting defects: clipped/overlapping text, broken tables, unreadable characters, or default-template styling.
- Charts, tables, and visuals must be legible in rendered pages with correct alignment.
- Use ASCII hyphens only. Avoid U+2011 (non-breaking hyphen) and other Unicode dashes.
- Citations and references must be human-readable; never leave tool tokens or placeholder strings.

## Final checks
- Re-render and inspect every page at 100% zoom before final delivery.
- Fix any spacing, alignment, or pagination issues and repeat the render loop.
- Confirm there are no leftovers (temp files, duplicate renders) unless the user asks to keep them.
```
</details></tool-use>

```
Editable working document for keynote development
Who Will Ultimately Control the AI Infrastructure that Higher Education Is Coming to Depend On?
Deep research dossier and keynote drafting pack
Prepared as a full briefing pack: conference reading, abstract stress-test, annotated concept notebook, evidence base, policy agenda, slide storyboard, and Q&A backup.
Core framing The conference is asking how higher education should use AI. This keynote asks who will control the infrastructure on which those uses depend.

Conference
Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026, Teesside University
Conference dates
15–17 April 2026
Programme slot
Day 1 keynote, 1:30–2:15 pm, immediately before the panel on reimagining the purpose and promise of universities
Purpose
To give you a full set of notes, definitions, evidence, cautions, policy levers, slide architecture, and Q&A backup for drafting the keynote
Last updated
17 March 2026

Use this pack as raw material. It is not a finished script; it is a deliberately over-complete set of arguments, evidence, examples, cautions, and speaking lines you can cut down into a 45-minute keynote.

Contents
• 1. Executive synthesis
• 2. Reading the room: conference themes, programme tensions, and audience implications
• 3. Stress-testing your abstract
• 4. Annotated concept notebook: every note, definition, and linked idea expanded
• 5. Evidence base for the keynote
• 6. Strategic synthesis: the position your keynote can own
• 7. Practical policy agenda for universities
• 8. Suggested slide architecture and speaking notes
• 9. Backup Q&A
• 10. Coverage map of your original notes and selected sources
This dossier is designed to be worked with quickly: every main section ends with implications for the talk, and the later sections translate the research into slides, lines, and likely answers.

1. Executive synthesis
The conference programme is rich in pedagogy, assessment, ethics, literacy, and practice. Your keynote can do something different and complementary: make the infrastructure question explicit. The strongest version of the talk is not anti-AI and not anti-industry. It is anti-passivity. It argues that universities are moving from occasional tool use to structural dependence, and that once AI is infrastructural the key question shifts from capability to control, accountability, resilience, and bargaining power.
The central strategic move is to reframe adoption as governance. Instead of asking only whether a model is impressive, cheap, or convenient, the talk asks who shapes the defaults built into teaching, assessment, advising, analytics, and institutional decision-making. That reframe allows you to connect seemingly separate topics in the programme—assessment redesign, learner analytics, AI literacy, ethics, accessibility, vendor platforms, and institutional strategy—into one coherent argument.
The talk works best as a three-step argument. First, AI is already becoming invisible infrastructure in higher education: student use is mainstream, assessment has already changed, and tools are being stitched into everyday workflows. Second, the infrastructure layer is highly concentrated, capital-intensive, and increasingly controlled by external firms whose incentives are not identical to the public mission of universities. Third, lock-in is happening now, while standards, contracts, norms, integrations, and expectations are still fluid. This is the small window in which universities can still move from passive consumption to active stewardship.
What the room most needs from this keynote Your keynote should sound like a governance keynote for a room that has mostly been invited to think about pedagogy, productivity, and assessment. That difference is your advantage. It lets you connect the whole programme rather than compete with the sessions already focused on tools.
Three claims worth making clearly and early
• AI in higher education is no longer just a set of optional tools. It is becoming part of the substrate through which teaching, assessment, support, and governance are delivered.
• Infrastructure matters because it shapes what institutions can see, do, audit, export, contest, and change. Control does not have to mean owning every component, but it does require legibility, bargaining power, portability, and evaluation capacity.
• The practical choice is not between total in-house sovereignty and total outsourcing. It is between institutions that remain capable stewards of their AI stack and institutions that become captive customers inside an extractive ecosystem.
The overall tone to strike
• Serious, practical, and public-interest oriented rather than apocalyptic.
• Critical of structural dependency, 
... (truncated)
```

```
6. Strategic synthesis: the position your keynote can own
The strongest keynote position sits between two inadequate poles. On one side is institutional boosterism: adopt faster, integrate more deeply, and trust the market to deliver what universities need. On the other side is total refusal: treat generative AI as inherently corrosive and aim mainly to resist it. Your abstract already suggests a more useful third position: engaged stewardship.
The third position in one paragraph
Engaged stewardship means universities neither retreat from AI nor surrender to it. They accept that AI will be part of the operating environment, but they refuse to treat vendor defaults as destiny. They build enough institutional capability to evaluate systems, negotiate terms, protect educational data, preserve exit options, and collaborate across the sector where single institutions are too small to act alone. They may buy some things, build some things, adapt some things, and reject some things—but they do so from a position of deliberate governance rather than dependency by drift.
Why this position is stronger than generic ethics talk
• It turns values into decisions. Ethics alone can remain decorative; procurement, data terms, evaluation, and architectural choices are operational.
• It speaks to the real structure of the ecosystem. Concentrated markets and capital asymmetries mean that institutions need bargaining strategy, not just aspiration.
• It matches the mood of the conference. The room is practical, not theoretical; stewardship gives a constructive answer without sounding naive.
• It keeps students and academic purpose at the centre. Infrastructure is judged by whom it serves and under what conditions.
A possible high-level formulation for the keynote
AI is becoming part of higher education's invisible infrastructure. That fact changes the question universities must ask. The issue is no longer simply whether a given tool is impressive or convenient. The issue is who controls the stack on which teaching, assessment, support, and governance increasingly rely. Because that stack is concentrated, expensive, and often externally owned, universities are now choosing whether to become passive customers in an extractive ecosystem or active stewards of the systems they depend on. The window for making that choice is small because standards, contracts, data arrangements, and institutional norms are hardening now.
A useful definition of control Control does not mean owning every layer. It means being able to understand, evaluate, contest, negotiate, and, when necessary, switch or reconfigure the systems on which core university functions depend.
The conceptual arc in one line
Claim type
How it functions in the talk
Descriptive claim
AI has shifted from optional toolset to infrastructural layer.
Political-economic claim
That layer is disproportionately owned and shaped by actors whose incentives are not identical to universities' public missions.
Temporal claim
Path dependence means that choices made during this fluid period may lock in future dependency.
Normative claim
Universities therefore need stewardship capacity: procurement discipline, data governance, evaluation, resilience, and collaboration.
Educational claim
The goal is not control for its own sake but accountability to learning, academic values, and student interests.
Lines that can do a lot of work
• Most infrastructure is invisible until it breaks, becomes expensive, or answers to someone else.
• The real issue is not whether AI enters higher education. It already has. The issue is on whose terms.
• A university that cannot evaluate the systems it depends on is not governing its AI future; it is renting one.
• The alternative to passive dependence is not heroic self-sufficiency. It is practical stewardship.
• The point of public-interest AI in universities is not to win a race with hyperscalers. It is to keep core educational systems legible, portable, governable, and aligned with purpose.

7. Practical policy agenda for universities
This section translates the argument into institutional moves. The point is not to produce a maximal wish-list, but to define the practical capacities that distinguish a steward from a passive customer. For each area, the keynote can offer a minimum move now, a stronger medium-term move, and a long-term direction.
7.1 Procurement: treat buying decisions as governance decisions
AI procurement is one of the most under-discussed control points in higher education. If procurement is weak, ethics remain rhetorical. Contracts and purchasing criteria determine which models are used, how data flow, what can be audited, which features can be switched off, whether outputs can be exported, and what happens when services change or
... (truncated)
```

```
8. Suggested slide architecture and speaking notes
Your slot is forty-five minutes, post-lunch, and positioned before a panel on the purpose of universities. That argues for a structure of roughly 12–14 slides with a clear narrative spine, generous pace, and minimal conceptual clutter. Start concrete, climb to the structural argument, and land in institutional choices.
Recommended narrative arc
• Visible failure: make invisible infrastructure concrete.
• Mainstream adoption: show that AI is already embedded in student and staff practice.
• Concentration and incentives: explain why dependence matters.
• Lock-in window: argue that choices made now will harden.
• Governance response: move from diagnosis to stewardship.
• Public mission: end on the kind of university we want these systems to serve.
Three possible openings
Opening style
What it does
When to choose it
Operational opening
Begin with the Canvas/AWS outage or a similar service interruption
Best if you want the room to feel the infrastructure argument immediately and intuitively.
Economic opening
Begin with the AI investment surge and the idea that bubbles can build infrastructure
Best if you want to set up the political economy quickly and show that hype may still leave durable systems behind.
Lock-in opening
Begin with QWERTY/path dependence and the notion that early defaults are hard to unwind
Best if you want a more conceptual but still accessible opening that frames the small window for action.
Recommended 13-slide storyboard
Slide 1 — When invisible infrastructure fails
Purpose: make the theme concrete.
Evidence / content: brief Canvas/AWS or Teams example.
Visual idea: a simple outage/status image, or a minimalist title slide with one stark sentence.
Possible line: 'Most infrastructure is invisible until it breaks.'

Slide 2 — AI is becoming infrastructure, not just a tool
Purpose: state the thesis.
Evidence / content: your abstract sentence plus four functions—teach, assess, advise, govern.
Visual idea: a layered stack or four icons arranged as institutional functions.
Possible line: 'The issue is no longer whether AI is on campus. It is how deeply it is entering the substrate.'

Slide 3 — Adoption is already mainstream
Purpose: establish urgency.
Evidence / content: HEPI figures on student use and assessment change.
Visual idea: three large numbers only.
Possible line: 'This is not a niche behaviour. It is now normal student practice.'

Slide 4 — Governance is lagging
Purpose: show the gap.
Evidence / content: Jisc maturity framing and UNESCO policy immaturity.
Visual idea: two columns—high use, lower institutional maturity.
Possible line: 'We have mass adoption without equivalent stewardship.'

Slide 5 — The boom is building real infrastructure
Purpose: bring in the bubble argument without sounding dismissive.
Evidence / content: hyperscaler capex, strategic partnerships, datacentre build-out.
Visual idea: simple capex/market concentration callouts.
```

```
9. Backup Q&A
These are the questions most likely to arise from leaders, practitioners, and sceptics in the room. Each answer is written in a way that can be spoken briefly and then expanded if needed.
Are you saying universities should build everything themselves?
No. The alternative to passive dependence is not building every layer in-house. It is preserving enough understanding, bargaining power, evaluation capacity, and exit options that core educational functions are not wholly governed by external defaults.

Isn't this just anti-vendor politics?
No. Universities will continue to work with vendors. The question is whether those relationships are structured as governable partnerships or as one-way dependencies. Good vendors should welcome clearer standards, stronger evaluation, and better institutional capability.

Can smaller or open models really compete with the frontier?
Not on every generic benchmark. But they do not have to. For many institutional tasks, grounded and well-evaluated smaller systems can be more appropriate, more controllable, and more cost-effective than the largest general models.

Isn't buying from the market simply more realistic than trying to do all this locally?
Buying from the market is realistic. Buying carelessly is not. The point of stewardship is to buy intelligently, know the dependencies, protect data, require evidence, and collaborate where a single institution lacks scale.

Students are already using public tools anyway. Isn't this too late?
It is too late for a denial strategy and too early for resignation. Student use being mainstream is exactly why institutions need better guidance, clearer assessment design, approved options, and more visible governance now.

Won't tighter governance slow innovation?
Bad governance slows innovation by creating fear and inconsistency. Good governance speeds useful innovation because it clarifies what is allowed, what is risky, and what evidence is needed.

Why focus on control when universities are under huge financial pressure?
Because financial pressure can turn short-term convenience into long-term dependency. Control matters most when budgets are tight, because switching costs, opaque pricing, and hidden data terms become even more consequential.

Are you underestimating how fast frontier models are improving?
No. Faster improvement is actually part of the argument. When capabilities move quickly, institutions need more evaluation and stronger architectural discipline, not less, because today's product may rely on tomorrow's unknown upstream changes.

What would one small university realistically do first?
Start with an AI dependency map, a procurement checklist, simple data rules, and one evaluation routine for a high-priority use case. Then look for partners rather than acting alone on everything.

How do we use institutional data ethically for fine-tuning or retrieval?
By being selective and governed: de-identify where possible, separate operational data from training data, obtain the right legal and ethical basis, involve oversight, and avoid treating student work as free raw material.

Is the real problem AI, or the wider platform model of higher education technology?
It is both. AI intensifies a pre-existing platform and cloud dependency problem. That is why the keynote talks about invisible infrastructure rather than about a single tool category.

What should students hear in this argument?
That the issue is not simply whether they use AI. It is whether the systems shaping their education are fair, transparent, governable, and accountable to their interests rather than only to vendor incentives.

How do you measure success if an institution takes stewardship seriously?
Success looks like clearer approved-use pathways, fewer unmanaged risky uses, better evidence on what works, stronger contractual terms, greater resilience, and decisions that remain visibly tied to educational purpose.

Where does academic freedom fit into this?
Academic freedom matters, but it sits inside institutional responsibility. Stewardship should widen meaningful choice and support critical use, not impose a single approved way of thinking or teaching.

A one-minute answer if someone asks, 'So what is your actual proposal?'
My proposal is that universities stop treating AI adoption as mainly a matter of guidance and start treating it as a matter of stewardship. That means buying more carefully, governing educational data more seriously, building evaluation capability, preserving architectural options, and collaborating across the sector where no single institution has enough leverage. The aim is not to own everything. It is to ensure that the systems higher education comes to depend on remain accountable to academic purpose and student interests.
```

```
Conceptual synthesis
Taken together, these concepts produce a coherent worldview for the keynote. The AI boom may build real infrastructure, but bubbles do not determine governance. Path dependence means early choices harden quickly. Lessig explains why architecture regulates behaviour. The viable system model shows that institutions need more than frontline experimentation; they need coordination, foresight, and mission-level steering. Surveillance capitalism and extractivism explain why apparently neutral data practices carry political and economic consequences. Grounding debates and fine-tuning research suggest that bigger is not always better for institutional use. And the educational perspective—creative work as medium, learning as more than answer retrieval, tasks as distinct from purposes—keeps the whole argument anchored in what universities are for.

5. Evidence base for the keynote
This section gives you the factual backbone for the talk. It is organised around the four claims most worth evidencing: AI use is already mainstream in higher education; institutional governance is lagging; the underlying infrastructure is concentrated and expensive; and meaningful alternatives exist through evaluation, grounding, and selective local or sectoral capability.
5.1 Adoption is ahead of governance
The headline educational fact is that student use is already mainstream. The 2026 HEPI student survey reports that 95 percent of UK full-time undergraduates use AI in at least one way and 94 percent use generative AI to help with assessed work. The same survey reports that 65 percent think assessment has changed significantly in response to AI. The 2025 HEPI survey already showed similar momentum one year earlier, along with limited training and mixed institutional encouragement.
The governance fact is that policy and organisational maturity are not keeping pace. Jisc's AI maturity work suggests most tertiary institutions are still in an experimental phase, and its governance guidance warns that when AI is left to chance, adoption fragments and risk rises. UNESCO's higher-education survey found that formal institutional policy remains uneven, with many institutions still uncertain or developing frameworks rather than operating settled governance regimes.
```

```
164:Deep dive: The deep importance of the example is that infrastructure risk is not only about outages or price rises. 168:Lessig's four modalities of regulation: law, norms, market, and code
169:Definition: Lawrence Lessig's key insight is that behaviour in digital environments is regulated by at least four interacting forces: law, social norms, markets, and code or architecture. Code matters because it determines what is possible, easy, difficult, visible, or impossible. Architecture is therefore not neutral background; it is a regulator.
170:Deep dive: This framework is particularly useful for higher-education AI because universities often talk as if policy alone will solve governance. Lessig helps show why policy documents are only one modality. Law may set obligations, but market concentration limits choices, local norms affect what staff and students actually do, and code determines defaults such as data retention, logging, explainability, exportability, and access control.
171:How it helps the keynote: Use Lessig as the bridge between abstract ethics and concrete system design. It lets you say that procurement is governance because procurement shapes code; code then shapes behaviour.
```

The dossier is basically the master briefing pack behind the talk, not a script. Its core position is very explicit: the keynote should be neither boosterish nor anti-AI, but argue for `engaged stewardship`. The central reframing is from `adoption` to `governance`: not “should HE use AI?” but “who controls the stack HE is coming to depend on?” That matches the current deck closely.

A few things are especially useful for working on the presentation content:
- The dossier says the talk should function as the programme’s `infrastructure argument` in a room otherwise focused on pedagogy, assessment, ethics, and practice.
- It recommends a clear third position: neither total outsourcing nor total self-build, but enough capability to evaluate, negotiate, preserve exit options, and collaborate.
- It provides the evidence bank behind the current stats and examples: HEPI 2025/2026 adoption figures, Jisc maturity/governance framing, UNESCO policy immaturity, Canvas/AWS and Teams outage examples, cloud/model concentration, and the case for smaller grounded systems plus evaluation.
- It also gives the policy spine that shows up in the back half of the deck: procurement, data governance, evaluation, resilience/modularity, collaboration, and student rights.

The most important mismatch between dossier and live deck is structural. The dossier recommends a `12–14 slide`, post-lunch keynote that opens concrete and gets to the infrastructure argument quickly. The live deck has become a `65-slide` granular version of that argument. That is not necessarily wrong, but it means the current deck is much more detailed and rhetorically layered than the dossier originally advised. In particular, the dossier recommends opening with an outage, economic build-out, or lock-in frame, whereas the live deck currently opens with the cover, three credential/title cards, three one-word statement slides, and then the thesis. That is a much slower runway.

So yes, I now have the extra context I need. The dossier gives us the underlying strategy, the evidence logic, and the intended conference fit; the live deck is the expanded execution of that. The biggest improvement lever is likely to tighten the opening and make the current slide sequence behave more like the dossier’s recommended keynote arc without losing the richer material.

_**Agent (gpt-5.4 2026-04-03 09:34:26Z)**_

Yes. I think the opening is defensible, and the logic is stronger now that I know what those slides are doing.

The good part is that the intro is not generic biography. It establishes two kinds of authority that matter for this talk: upstream technical understanding and downstream institutional practice. That is a strong combination for a keynote about infrastructure. The three lens slides also work conceptually: `transparency`, `distortion`, and `extraction` are memorable, and they give the rest of the talk a frame richer than simple pro/anti-AI positioning.

My main comments are about clarity and pacing, not the underlying idea.

First, the credentials need to read as `why this speaker can see the whole stack`, not just `about me`. Right now the sequence can do that, but only if your spoken transition is explicit. I would make the bridge very clear: “I come at this from two directions: I’ve worked on AI itself, and I’ve spent a lot of time looking at what AI does inside education institutions.” Without that line, three intro cards in a row risk feeling ornamental.

Second, the two expertise slides want a bit more specificity in the slide text itself. `Distributed Agents for Autonomous Spacecraft` and `Free Text Analysis with Student Voice AI` mean something, but they do not yet say why they matter to this argument. Even a short subtitle on each would help. For example: one slide says `technical grounding in AI systems`; the other says `practical grounding in institutional educational data`. That would make the opening do argumentative work, not just credential work.

Third, the three lens slides are strong, but they are abstract enough that the audience will need the rule of use immediately. I would say explicitly: “These are the three things I want you to watch for in every claim about AI in higher education.” Otherwise they may sound like three standalone provocations rather than a framework for the talk.

So my bottom line is: keep the opening architecture, but tighten the causal link. The opening works if it clearly says:
1. why you are credible,
2. why those two backgrounds belong together,
3. why `transparency`, `distortion`, and `extraction` are the lenses for everything that follows.

One small factual note: the deck currently says `Dr Stuart Grey`, not `Gray`, in the live [content.json](/Users/stugrey/Documents/Student Voice ICD/Conferences:Webinars/Teesside Keynote/deck-generator/content.json). If `Gray` was intentional, that wants correcting before we touch anything else.

```diff
+const fs = require("fs");

+  N("Move from the big question to why I am the person making this argument.", [

+  N("Take the first half of that perspective: technical grounding.", [

+  N("Then pivot from technical grounding to applied institutional grounding.", [

+  ]),
+  N("Now give the room the first of three lenses for reading the rest of the talk.", [

+    "Deliver it as a clean beat, not a full explanation yet.",

+  N("Add the second lens by shifting from visibility to shape.", [
+    "Distortion asks what gets bent when systems reward what is easy to capture, measure, or automate.",
+    "This will later connect to assessment design, pedagogy, workflow simplification, and institutional incentives.",

+  N("Complete the triad with the political-economy lens.", [

+  N("Having set up the lenses, move into the first descriptive claim.", [

+    "Use the subtitle to tell the audience we are now moving from framing to diagnosis.",
+    "The job here is to make 'infrastructure' feel concrete.",
+  ]),
+  N("Start with the broad institutional picture before going to evidence.", [

+    "Stress the shift from optional use to embedded workflow.",
+    "Say that once many activities assume the system is there, AI stops behaving like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.",

+  N("Back the previous claim with numbers so the room feels the urgency.", [

+  N("Now make infrastructure tangible by talking about failure.", [

+    "Use it to set up the next question: if control matters, who is actually building and owning the infrastructure?",

+  N("Land the contrast in one phrase.", [

+  N("Explain adoption more realistically.", [
+    "People reach for AI through motivation, capability, and trust.",
+    "Governance fails if it treats use as something controlled only by rules.",
+    "This is a useful slide for showing that stewardship includes culture and support, not just restriction.",
+  ]),
+  N("Translate stewardship into organisational form.", [
+    "The institution needs a viable system for AI governance, not just a policy document.",
+    "Say that many campuses are rich in experimentation at the edge and thin in coordination, assurance, foresight, and mission-level steering.",
+    "This is the plain-language version of the viable system diagnosis.",

+  N("Bring the procurement lesson home.", [

+  N("Broaden from buying rules to technical design.", [

+    "Use modularity, plural model exposure, and right-sized models as practical design principles.",
+    "This is where the audience should hear that architecture choices can preserve agency.",
+  ]),
+  N("Compress the stewardship agenda into something memorable.", [
+    "Five capabilities, three default questions, one governing standard.",
+    "Say that institutions do not need one hundred principles; they need a small set of capabilities used consistently.",
+    "This slide should make the constructive section feel manageable.",
+  ]),
+  N("Finish the section by scaling outward.", [
+    "Single universities are too small to negotiate, evaluate, and build everything alone.",
+    "Sector collaboration and public alternatives are not idealism; they are a realistic response to asymmetric markets.",
+    "This lets you sound practical without sounding institutionally isolated.",
+  ]),
+  N("State the positive identity of the argument.", [
+    "The alternative to passive dependence is practical stewardship.",
+    "This line matters because it keeps the keynote from sounding like a sovereignty fantasy.",
+    "Pause slightly; this is the constructive counterpart to the earlier warnings.",
+  ]),
+  N("Move from strategy to consequences.", [
+    "This final section asks what the next decade looks like under two different patterns of choice.",
+    "Use the subtitle to frame the end as a genuine fork, not a foregone conclusion.",
+    "The tone here should become slightly more civic and decision-oriented.",
+  ]),
+  N("Paint the better future concretely.", [
+    "A good future is not universities beating hyperscalers at their own game.",
+    "It is cheaper infrastructure on better terms, human-centred pedagogy, and stronger public capability.",
+    "Keep the picture specific enough to feel plausible rather than utopian.",
+  ]),
+  N("Then contrast it with the quieter bad future.", [
+    "The captured future is not dramatic collapse; it is ongoing functioning under worsening dependency.",
+    "Emphasise rent extraction, thinner provision, and opaque governance.",
+    "The point is to show what slow loss of autonomy looks like in ordinary institutional life.",
+  ]),
+  N("Convert the contrast into leadership choices.", [
+    "Every institution has to decide what it will refuse to outsource by default.",
+    "Use the three questions as decision tests: what must we retain control over, what should we build or evaluate together, and what rights should students have?",
+    "This sets up the closing agenda cleanly.",
+  ]),
+  N("Make the end operational.", [
+    "The Monday-morning agenda shows that stewardship begins with unglamorous but doable steps.",
+    "Map dependence, tighten the basics, and act collectively.",
+    "This is where the talk leaves the audience with something they can do next week.",
+  ]),
+  N("Lift back from the checklist to the stakes.", [
+    "The point is bigger than whether to deploy a particular tool.",
+    "Universities are helping set the defaults that will structure higher education for years.",
+    "This is the right slide to widen the frame before the final question.",
+  ]),
+  N("End with the question rather than another answer.", [
+    "Let the closing question hang for a moment and hand neatly into discussion or panel.",
+    "The point is to force the room to connect infrastructure control with the kind of university it wants to make possible.",
+    "Do not rush the last line; it is the civic and institutional landing point.",
+  ]),
+];
+
+if (notes.length !== content.slides.length) {
+  throw new Error(`Expected ${content.slides.length} note blocks, found ${notes.length}`);
+}
+
+const original = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(content));
+content.slides = content.slides.map((slide, index) => ({
+  ...slide,
+  notes: notes[index],
+}));
+
+const stripNotes = (value) =>
+  JSON.parse(
+    JSON.stringify(value, (key, innerValue) => (key === "notes" ? undefined : innerValue)),
+  );
+
+if (JSON.stringify(stripNotes(content)) !== JSON.stringify(stripNotes(original))) {
+  throw new Error("Validation failed: fields other than 'notes' changed.");
+}
+
+fs.writeFileSync(filePath, `${JSON.stringify(content, null, 2)}\n`);
+console.log(`Added notes to ${content.slides.length} slides in ${filePath}`);
```

```
/** Mark content as dirty and enable the save button */
function markDirty() {

/** Build the collapsible edit panel for a slide */
function buildEditPanel(slide, slideIndex) {
  const fields = EDITABLE_FIELDS[slide.type] || [];
  const panel = document.createElement('div');
  panel.className = 'slide-edit-panel';
  panel.style.display = 'none';

  // Top-level text fields
  fields.forEach(key => {
    const val = slide[key] ?? '';
    const isLong = key === 'body' || key === 'text';

    const group = document.createElement('div');
    group.className = 'edit-group';

    const label = document.createElement('label');
    label.className = 'edit-label';
    label.textContent = key;

    const input = isLong
      ? document.createElement('textarea')
      : document.createElement('input');
    input.className = 'edit-input';
    input.value = val;
    if (isLong) input.rows = 3;

    input.addEventListener('input', () => {
      currentContent.slides[slideIndex][key] = input.value;
      markDirty();
      renderSingleSlide(slideIndex);
    });

    group.appendChild(label);
    group.appendChild(input);
    panel.appendChild(group);
  });

  // Nested array fields: points[] for content, stats[] for data
  if (slide.type === 'content' && slide.points) {
    slide.points.forEach((point, pi) => {
      const header = document.createElement('div');
      header.className = 'edit-nested-header';
      header.textContent = `Point ${pi + 1}`;
      panel.appendChild(header);

      ['title', 'description'].forEach(key => {
        const group = document.createElement('div');
        group.className = 'edit-group';

        const label = document.createElement('label');
        label.className = 'edit-label';
        label.textContent = key;

        const isLong = key === 'description';
        const input = isLong
          ? document.createElement('textarea')
          : document.createElement('input');
        input.className = 'edit-input';
        input.value = point[key] ?? '';
        if (isLong) input.rows = 2;

        input.addEventListener('input', () => {
          currentContent.slides[slideIndex].points[pi][key] = input.value;
          markDirty();
          renderSingleSlide(slideIndex);
        });

        group.appendChild(label);
        group.appendChild(input);
        panel.appendChild(group);
      });
    });
  }

  if (slide.type === 'data' && slide.stats) {
    slide.stats.forEach((stat, si) => {
      const header = document.createElement('div');
      header.className = 'edit-nested-header';
      header.textContent = `Stat ${si + 1}`;
      panel.appendChild(header);

      ['value', 'label'].forEach(key => {
        const group = document.createElement('div');
        group.className = 'edit-group';

        const label = document.createElement('label');
        label.className = 'edit-label';
        label.textContent = key;

        const input = document.createElement('input');
        input.className = 'edit-input';
        input.value = stat[key] ?? '';

        input.addEventListener('input', () => {
          currentContent.slides[slideIndex].stats[si][key] = input.value;
          markDirty();
          renderSingleSlide(slideIndex);
        });

        group.appendChild(label);
        group.appendChild(input);
        panel.appendChild(group);
      });
    });
  }

  return panel;
}

  const inner = slideEl.querySelector('.slide-inner');
```

```
/** Relative luminance from an rgb() or rgba() string */
function luminance(cssColor) {
  const match = cssColor.match(/(\d+),\s*(\d+),\s*(\d+)/);
  if (!match) return 0;
  const [r, g, b] = [match[1], match[2], match[3]].map(c => {
    const s = Number(c) / 255;
    return s <= 0.03928 ? s / 12.92 : Math.pow((s + 0.055) / 1.055, 2.4);
  });
  return 0.2126 * r + 0.7152 * g + 0.0722 * b;
}

/** Scale stat values so the longest text fills its block width, and all others match that size */
function fitStatSizes() {
  document.querySelectorAll('.data-big-numbers').forEach(slide => {
    const values = slide.querySelectorAll('.stat-value');
    if (values.length === 0) return;

    // Reset and prevent wrapping for measurement
    values.forEach(v => { v.style.fontSize = ''; v.style.whiteSpace = 'nowrap'; });

    // Find the size that makes the longest text exactly fill its block
    let minFitted = Infinity;
    values.forEach(v => {
      const block = v.closest('.stat-block');
      const padL = parseFloat(getComputedStyle(block).paddingLeft);
      const padR = parseFloat(getComputedStyle(block).paddingRight);
      const available = block.clientWidth - padL - padR;
      const currentSize = parseFloat(getComputedStyle(v).fontSize);
      const textWidth = v.scrollWidth;
      if (textWidth > 0) {
        minFitted = Math.min(minFitted, currentSize * (available / textWidth));
      }
    });

    if (minFitted !== Infinity) {
      // Cap at 55% of block height so label text still fits
      const firstBlock = values[0].closest('.stat-block');
      const blockH = firstBlock.clientHeight - parseFloat(getComputedStyle(firstBlock).paddingTop) - parseFloat(getComputedStyle(firstBlock).paddingBottom);
      const finalSize = Math.min(minFitted, blockH * 0.55);
      values.forEach(v => v.style.fontSize = `${finalSize}px`);
    }
  });
}

/** Set adaptive text colour on stat blocks and add border when block disappears into slide bg */
function fixStatBlockContrast() {
  document.querySelectorAll('.stat-block').forEach(block => {
    const blockL = luminance(getComputedStyle(block).backgroundColor);
    block.style.color = blockL > 0.18 ? '#1B2A3D' : '#FFFFFF';

    // Add border when block blends into slide background
    const slide = block.closest('.slide-inner');
    if (slide) {
      const slideL = luminance(getComputedStyle(slide).backgroundColor);
      const contrast = Math.abs(blockL - slideL);
      block.style.border = contrast < 0.05 ? '1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.15)' : '';
    }
  });
}
```

```diff
+  notePointsLabel.className = 'edit-label';
+  notePointsLabel.textContent = 'points (one bullet per line)';
+
+  const notePointsInput = document.createElement('textarea');
+  notePointsInput.className = 'edit-input';
+  notePointsInput.rows = Math.max(4, notes.points.length || 0);
+  notePointsInput.value = notes.points.join('\n');
+  notePointsInput.placeholder = 'Key ideas to hit while speaking.';
+  notePointsInput.addEventListener('input', () => {
+    ensureSlideNotes(currentContent.slides[slideIndex]).points = notePointsInput.value
+      .split('\n')
+      .map(line => line.trim())
+      .filter(Boolean);
+    markDirty();
+  });
+
+  notePointsGroup.appendChild(notePointsLabel);
+  notePointsGroup.appendChild(notePointsInput);
+  panel.appendChild(notePointsGroup);
 
   return panel;
 }
```
</details></tool-use>
